title: Asymmetric Encoder-Decoder
concept: MAE uses a large encoder only on visible patches, and a lightweight decoder on all tokens, saving massive computation during pre-training.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the pretext task and loss function used in Masked Autoencoders?

options.text: Pixel reconstruction optimized via MSE loss.
Contrastive matching optimized via InfoNCE loss.

exercises.explain: Correct! MAE predicts missing pixels and grades itself using MSE loss.

cards.title: The heavy lifter
cards.body: If you mask 75% of an image, you don't want to run a massive, expensive model on all those blank squares. MAE uses a brilliant trick: it uses a heavy model only on the 25% visible patches, and a tiny, lightweight model to paint the rest. Let's explore this asymmetric design.

cards.title: Asymmetric Architecture
cards.body: MAE is asymmetric. The heavy encoder only processes the 25% visible patches. Afterward, we insert learnable, shared 'mask tokens' for the hidden slots, combine them with the encoder outputs, and pass the full sequence (100% of tokens) through a very lightweight decoder.

cards.title: Architect and draftsperson
cards.body: Imagine building a house. The senior architect (heavy encoder) only looks at the core structural demands (visible patches) to plan the layout. They hand this plan to a junior draftsperson (lightweight decoder), who does the repetitive work of coloring in the wallpaper and tiles (masked patches).

cards.title: Compute savings
cards.body: Because the encoder makes up 90% of the model's compute and only processes 25% of the tokens, MAE pre-training runs up to **4 times faster** and consumes significantly less GPU memory than symmetric models.

cards.title: Decoder is discarded!
cards.body: You might think the decoder is a permanent part of the model. In reality, once pre-training is complete, **the decoder is discarded entirely**! We only keep the trained encoder to deploy on our target classification or detection tasks.

left.text:
Only visible patches (e.g. 49 tokens)
Processed by heavy ViT encoder
Extracts rich semantic features

right.text:
Visible embeddings + Mask tokens
Processed by light ViT decoder
Reconstructs raw 2D pixel grid

exercises.prompt: Why does the MAE encoder only process the unmasked (visible) patches?
exercises.explain: Processing only 25% of tokens in the heavy encoder reduces pre-training compute demands by roughly 3-4x.

options.text: To save massive amounts of computational power and memory during pre-training
Because masked patches contain corrupted color channels
To prevent the model from using position embeddings
Because the encoder can only handle sequences shorter than 50 tokens

exercises.prompt: True or false: Once pre-training is complete, the lightweight decoder is kept and deployed along with the encoder.
exercises.explain: False. The decoder is discarded after pre-training; downstream tasks only use the pre-trained encoder.
The decoder contains fewer layers and channels, making it computationally light.
exercises.sentence: The MAE architecture is asymmetric because it uses a heavy encoder and a ___ decoder.

exercises.prompt: How does the decoder process the missing patches that the encoder ignored?
exercises.explain: MAE inserts learnable mask tokens as placeholders for the missing slots, allowing the decoder to predict pixels at those coordinates.

options.text: By inserting a shared, learnable 'mask token' vector into every missing patch slot
By copy-pasting the pixels of the nearest visible patch
By bypassing those coordinates completely during reconstruction

exercises.prompt: If the decoder is lightweight, can it still produce good reconstructions?

options.text: Yes — because the heavy encoder has already done the hard work of extracting the semantic context, leaving the decoder to handle simple pixel filling.
No — the reconstructions are always completely black.
Yes — but only if we train without position embeddings.

exercises.explain: Outstanding! The encoder extracts the deep concepts, so the lightweight decoder just needs to translate those concepts back to pixels. Next: why high mask ratios improve features!
